description: Tomato yellow leaf curl virus is a serious viral disease transmitted primarily by whiteflies. Early infection can severely stunt plants and greatly reduce fruit production.
symptoms: Yellowing between or along leaf margins
Leaves may become smaller than normal
Shortened internodes may produce a bushy appearance
Fruit production can be severely reduced

favorable_conditions.humidity: Indirect effect through whitefly population dynamics
favorable_conditions.additional_conditions: Conditions favoring whitefly populations increase virus transmission risk

spread: Movement of infected plant material can introduce the virus
Infected host plants can serve as virus reservoirs
prevention: Use resistant or tolerant tomato varieties where available
Remove confirmed infected plants when appropriate
Manage weeds and alternative hosts according to local recommendations
Use physical exclusion methods such as insect-proof netting in suitable protected cultivation systems

immediate_actions: Remove confirmed severely infected plants where appropriate
Manage whitefly populations to reduce further virus transmission
cultural_control: Use physical barriers in protected cultivation where feasible
biological_control: Conserve or introduce appropriate whitefly natural enemies in suitable integrated pest management systems

chemical_control.recommended: There is no chemical treatment that cures TYLCV-infected plants. Locally registered insecticides may be used as part of integrated whitefly management to reduce vector populations.
chemical_control.warning: Do not present insecticide treatment as a cure for the virus. Whiteflies can develop insecticide resistance, so follow local integrated pest-management and resistance-management recommendations.

severity_guidance.low: A suspected symptomatic plant should be inspected for whiteflies and monitored closely.
severity_guidance.moderate: Multiple symptomatic plants with whitefly activity indicate increased transmission risk.
severity_guidance.high: Widespread symptoms and high whitefly pressure can result in severe yield loss and require integrated vector and disease management.

farmer_advice: There is no cure for TYLCV after a plant becomes infected. Management should focus on resistant varieties, healthy transplants, early detection, removal of important infection sources, and integrated whitefly management.
disclaimer: Leaf curling and yellowing can also result from nutrient deficiencies, herbicide injury, environmental stress, or other viruses. Confirm uncertain cases before taking major crop-management decisions.
